nodes.title: être — to be

explanation.body: Être handles identity, description, and location. Age, hunger, thirst, and temperature use avoir instead — translating those word for word is the top mistake.

examples.french: Je suis fatigué. On est au café.
examples.english: I'm tired. We're at the café.
I'm hungry. I'm thirty. (avoir, not être)

explanation.body: Suis, es, est, and sont cover almost all spoken use. On est replaces nous sommes completely in conversation.
bullets: c'est = ce + est — every c'est you say is already être
nous sommes: exists in writing, rarely said aloud

explanation.body: Je suis shortens to j'suis, then often to one syllable: chuis. Tu es is nearly always t'es.
Use imparfait for states and descriptions — c'était covers almost every review of a meal, film, or weekend. Passé composé exists but fits only one-off events.

examples.english: It was great. Where were you?

nodes.street_notes: je suis → j'suis, often chuis in fast speech; tu es → t'es (near-universal); c'est carries most of the load in real speech.

nodes.title: avoir — to have

explanation.body: Avoir means 'to have', but French uses it where English uses 'be': age, hunger, thirst, fear, luck, need. Using être there is the most common English-speaker error.

examples.french: T'as faim ? Il a besoin de rien.
examples.english: Are you hungry? He doesn't need anything.

explanation.body: Il y a means 'there is/are' and, with a time expression, 'ago'. In speech it's almost always y'a.
Tu as becomes t'as in nearly every spoken sentence. Keeping the full form sounds stiff. J'ai stays j'ai — it's already short.
Most French past tenses are avoir + past participle. Control these six present forms and you unlock the passé composé of hundreds of verbs immediately.

examples.french: On a mangé. Ils ont rien dit.

nodes.street_notes: tu as → t'as (near-universal); il y a → y'a. j'ai stays j'ai.

nodes.title: aller — to go

explanation.body: Aller means 'to go'. It also builds the future: aller + infinitive is spoken French for 'going to'.
Vais, vas, and va cover je, tu, il, and on. Everything you say daily sounds like 'va'.

examples.french: je vais, tu vas, il va, on va

explanation.body: Ça va ? runs on aller. French asks how you're going, not how you are.
Aller is a movement verb, so the past uses être: je suis allé. Street says j'suis allé.

examples.french: On est allés au resto hier.
examples.english: We went to the restaurant yesterday.

nodes.street_notes: je vais → j'vais; ça va is the workhorse. Passé composé with être: je suis allé(e).

nodes.title: faire — to do / to make

explanation.body: Faire covers both 'do' and 'make'. When you'd use either English verb, faire is the French one.
Faire is irregular. Memorise fais, fait, and font — they cover almost everything you'll say.
bullets: je/tu fais, il/on fait, ils font
vous faites is the odd one out — nothing else ends -aites

explanation.body: Tu fais quoi ? is the default spoken question. No inversion, no est-ce que — just drop quoi at the end.

examples.french: Tu fais quoi là ? — Je fais rien.
examples.english: What are you doing? — I'm not doing anything.

explanation.body: Faire + infinitive means 'make someone do' or 'get something done'. French needs no extra word; faire carries both meanings alone.

examples.french: Il fait pleurer tout le monde.

examples.english: I'm going to get that fixed.

nodes.street_notes: je fais → j'fais; tu fais quoi ? is the default question form.

nodes.title: vouloir — to want

explanation.body: Vouloir means 'to want'. Put an infinitive after it for an action, or a noun for a thing. No 'de' or 'que' between vouloir and the infinitive — English pulls that reflex, French doesn't.

explanation.header: The stem splits in the middle
explanation.body: Je, tu, il, ils use veu-. Vous keeps the full voul-. Irregular, but it's the same split every time.

examples.french: j'veux, tu veux, il veut, ils veulent — vs — vous voulez

explanation.body: Je veux straight to someone you don't know sounds blunt. Swap in je voudrais for any request to a stranger or server.

examples.french: J'veux de l'eau. → Je voudrais de l'eau.
examples.english: I want water. → I'd like some water.

explanation.body: Passé composé = a specific decision or attempt. Imparfait = ongoing background want.

examples.french: J'ai voulu partir, mais j'ai raté le bus.
examples.english: I tried to leave, but I missed the bus.

examples.french: Je voulais te voir mais j'avais pas le temps.
examples.english: I wanted to see you but I didn't have time.

nodes.street_notes: je veux → j'veux; tu veux → tu veux (no contraction, consonant).

nodes.title: pouvoir — to be able to / can

explanation.body: Pouvoir means 'can' or 'be able to'. It covers both ability and permission. It always takes an infinitive directly — no preposition between them.
The stem splits. Singular and ils use peux/peut/peuvent; vous gets pouvez. The x on je and tu mirrors vouloir — learn them as a pair.
Je drops its vowel in fast speech: j'peux, j'pouvais. J'peux pas is one of the most common refusals in spoken French — hear it and copy it.
Use j'ai pu for a specific moment you managed — or failed — to do something. Use je pouvais for ongoing past ability.

examples.english: I managed to talk to him yesterday.

examples.french: Avant, je pouvais courir des heures.
examples.english: I used to be able to run for hours.

nodes.street_notes: je peux → j'peux; 'je peux pas' → 'j'peux pas' is one of the most common street strings.

nodes.title: devoir — to have to / must

explanation.header: Must, have to, should — one verb
explanation.body: Devoir covers all three. The tense does the work: present = strong obligation, imparfait = 'was supposed to', conditional = soft 'should' (covered later).

examples.english: We were supposed to call him.

explanation.body: Devoir + infinitive expresses logical deduction, not just obligation. Context tells you which reading applies.

examples.english: He must be tired. (deduction, not obligation)
You must have made a mistake.

explanation.body: Doivent and dû are the stumbling points. Dû always carries the circumflex — it separates it from the article du.
Je dois compresses to j'dois in fast speech. For future obligation, j'vais devoir is more natural than je devrai.

examples.english: We're going to have to wait.

nodes.street_notes: je dois → j'dois.

nodes.title: savoir — to know (facts, how to)

explanation.body: French splits 'to know' in two. Savoir covers facts, information, and skills. Connaître covers familiarity with people and places.

examples.french: Je sais qu'il est parti. / Je connais Marie.
examples.english: I know he left. / I know Marie.

explanation.body: Savoir + infinitive means you have the ability. Drop 'comment' — adding it sounds bookish.

examples.french: Elle sait conduire. Tu sais cuisiner ?
examples.english: She can drive. Do you know how to cook?

explanation.body: Je sais pas drops the ne in normal speech, compresses to j'sais pas, then fuses into chais pas. The spelling captures the sound — it's not a separate word.

examples.french: Je ne sais pas → j'sais pas → chais pas

explanation.body: J'ai su means you found something out at a specific moment. Je savais means you were in an ongoing state of knowing.

examples.english: I found out the truth yesterday.

examples.french: Je savais pas que c'était fermé.
examples.english: I didn't know it was closed.

nodes.street_notes: je sais pas → j'sais pas → chais pas (the app's namesake).

nodes.title: dire — to say / to tell

explanation.body: Dire is irregular in exactly one spot: vous dites, not vous disez. Every other irregular -re verb uses -ez, so this one stands alone — learn it cold.
Dire covers both. The trap: when 'tell' has a person after it, French uses an indirect pronoun — lui or leur — not a preposition.
Where English says 'you say', 'they say', or 'people say', French says on dit.

examples.french: On dit 'bonne chance' avant un exam.
examples.english: People say 'bonne chance' before an exam.

explanation.body: Je dis drops its vowel to j'dis in fast speech. In the passé composé, dit never changes form — no matter who's speaking.

nodes.street_notes: je dis → j'dis; on dit for general statements.

nodes.title: voir — to see

explanation.body: Voir covers both 'to see' and 'to see/meet a person'. Using rencontrer where voir belongs is the main English-speaker error.
Most forms use voi-. Vous and ils break to voy-: voyez, voient. The imparfait stays on voy- throughout.

examples.french: Ils voient tout. / Vous voyiez ça ?
examples.english: They see everything. / You were seeing that?

explanation.header: Vu: the past you'll use most
explanation.body: J'ai vu covers one-off events. T'as vu also works as a street filler meaning 'you know?' or 'right?' — you'll hear it at sentence edges constantly.

examples.french: T'as vu le film ? — Ouais, j'ai vu ça hier, t'as vu.
examples.english: Did you see the film? — Yeah, I saw it yesterday, you know.

explanation.body: Je drops to j' before vois and voyais. Tu vois alone, mid-sentence, is pure filler — not a real question.

examples.french: J'vois pas ce qu'il veut, tu vois.
examples.english: I don't see what he wants, you know.

nodes.street_notes: je vois → j'vois; tu vois as a filler ('you know').

drills.english: I see the problem.
Do you see what I mean?
drills.french_formal: Tu vois ce que je veux dire ?

drills.english: He sees her every day at the café.
drills.french_formal: Il la voit tous les jours au café.

nodes.title: prendre — to take

explanation.body: French uses prendre where English picks different verbs: ordering food, catching transport, making a turn.
Singular and on use prend-. Ils doubles the n — and that changes the vowel sound.
The past participle is pris. Memorise it once — it stays the same for every subject.

examples.english: What did you have? — I took the train.

explanation.body: Je loses its e before prends and prenais. Spoken French avoids two vowel sounds in a row.

nodes.title: venir — to come

explanation.body: Venir splits: vien- for je, tu, il, ils — ven- for vous. That split is the only hard thing; endings are standard.

examples.french: je viens / ils viennent — but vous venez
examples.english: note the double-n in viennent: that's the stem shift

explanation.body: English speakers reach for avoir — wrong. Venir takes être, so the past participle agrees with the subject.
bullets: Feminine subject → add -e: elle est venue
Plural subject → add -s: ils sont venus

examples.french: Elle est venue hier. Ils sont venus ensemble.
examples.english: She came yesterday. They came together.

explanation.body: Use passé composé for a specific arrival. Use imparfait for repeated past coming. Use futur proche for plans — that's almost always how French handles near-future movement.

examples.french: Il venait tous les étés. → On va venir ce soir.
examples.english: He used to come every summer. → We're coming tonight.

explanation.body: Je viens drops to j'viens in speech. Tu viens ? alone is an invitation — nothing else needed.

examples.french: T'es venu ou pas ? J'viens, attends.
examples.english: Did you come or not? I'm coming, hang on.

nodes.street_notes: je viens → j'viens; tu viens ? as a one-word invitation. Passé composé with être.

nodes.title: mettre — to put / to put on

explanation.body: Mettre means 'put', but English speakers miss two extra uses: putting on clothes and turning on a device or media.
bullets: put somewhere: mettre les clés sur la table
put on clothing: mettre un manteau
turn on / start: mettre la télé, mettre de la musique

explanation.body: The stem drops to met- for je, tu, il. Those three forms sound identical. Only mettez and mettent keep the double-t.

examples.english: What jacket are you putting on?

explanation.body: Mis is irregular — learn it once. It appears often in questions about time taken or where something was left.
Je mets drops its vowel to j'mets. You'll hear j'mets ça là as a quick stand-in for a whole description of action.

nodes.title: parler — to speak / to talk

explanation.body: Parler is the model for regular -er verbs — about 80% of French. Drop -er, add the endings.
bullets: Stem: parl-
Endings: -e, -es, -e, -e, -ez, -ent

explanation.body: Je/tu/il/on/ils all sound like parl. Only vous parlez sounds different. Context and pronouns tell you who's speaking.

examples.french: Il parle vite. / Ils parlent vite.
examples.english: He speaks fast. / They speak fast. — identical out loud.

explanation.body: Passé composé = finished event. Imparfait = habit or background. English speakers overuse passé composé — 'we always talked' needs imparfait.

examples.french: J'ai parlé au médecin ce matin.
examples.english: I talked to the doctor this morning.

examples.french: On parlait tout le temps de ça.

explanation.body: J'vais parler and t'as parlé are standard casual speech. The full forms sound stiff in conversation.

examples.english: I'm going to talk to him tomorrow.

nodes.street_notes: The model -er verb: one spoken form for je/tu/il/on/ils (parle/parles/parlent all sound identical).

nodes.title: aimer — to like / to love

explanation.body: Bare aimer means love for people, strong preference for things. Add bien to dial it back to ordinary liking.

examples.french: J'aime Marie. / J'aime bien ce quartier.
examples.english: I love Marie. / I like this neighbourhood.

explanation.body: Ils aiment ends in silent -ent, so j'aime and ils aiment sound identical. Context separates them.

examples.french: j'aime / tu aimes / il aime / ils aiment

explanation.body: Tu drops to t' before a vowel in fast speech. Je vais aimer compresses to j'vais aimer.

examples.french: T'aimes ça ? T'as aimé le film ?
examples.english: You like that? Did you like the film?

explanation.body: Use passé composé for a finished reaction. Use imparfait for an ongoing state or habit in the past.

nodes.title: acheter — to buy

explanation.body: Acheter has a silent e in its stem. When the ending adds no vowel sound, French adds è so that syllable stays audible.
bullets: è in: j'achète, tu achètes, il achète, ils achètent
plain e in: vous achetez (ending already voiced)

explanation.body: Use acheter whenever money changes hands for goods. English speakers default to prendre ('take') in shops — that's the mistake.

examples.english: I'm buying this. (not je prends ça)

examples.french: On a acheté des clopes au tabac.
examples.english: We bought cigarettes at the tabac.

explanation.body: T'achètes fully replaces tu achètes in casual speech. J'vais acheter is the default for any near-future plan.

examples.english: I'm going to grab some food.

explanation.body: Real purchases are completed events — passé composé dominates. Imparfait is for repeated past habit only.

examples.english: I used to buy everything online.

nodes.street_notes: Stem-change -er verb: è in the singular/ils forms (j'achète), plain e when the ending is pronounced (vous achetez).

nodes.title: manger — to eat

explanation.body: Manger follows the standard -er pattern. French keeps the g soft, so an e stays before any ending that would harden it.

examples.english: the e protects the soft g in every imparfait form

explanation.body: Passé composé = meal is done. Imparfait = habit or interrupted eating. Futur proche = any plan.

examples.french: Je mangeais quand il a appelé.
examples.english: I was eating when he called.

explanation.body: Rising intonation turns on mange ? into 'shall we eat?' Present tense doubles as a near-future invitation in spoken French.

examples.english: Shall we eat? — Yeah, I'm hungry.

explanation.body: T'as mangé compresses tu as. J'vais manger drops the e from je. Both are normal fast speech, not errors.

examples.french: J'vais manger un truc vite fait.
examples.english: I'm going to grab something quick.

nodes.street_notes: on mange ? as an invitation. Imparfait keeps the e: je mangeais.

nodes.title: partir — to leave

explanation.body: Partir means you're leaving — it never takes an object. If you name a place you're leaving, use quitter instead.
Most -ir verbs keep a long stem, but partir shrinks to par- in the singular. The plural restores the t.

examples.french: Il part à midi. / Ils partent demain.
examples.english: He's leaving at noon. / They're leaving tomorrow.

explanation.body: Partir uses être in the passé composé, so the past participle agrees with the subject. Add -e for feminine, -s for plural.
On part ? with rising intonation means 'shall we go?' — it's the everyday way to suggest leaving.

examples.french: On part ? — Ouais, j'vais partir dans cinq minutes.
examples.english: Shall we go? — Yeah, I'm leaving in five minutes.

nodes.street_notes: je pars → j'pars; on part ? = shall we go? Passé composé with être: je suis parti(e).

nodes.title: finir — to finish

explanation.body: Finir is the model regular -ir verb. Plural forms add -iss-; singular forms don't.
Use passé composé for one finished event. Use imparfait for repeated or ongoing past action. English speakers overuse passé composé — that's the one mistake to avoid.
C'est fini means 'it's over' or 'it's done' — for anything, not conjugated for person. Futur proche contracts: j'vais finir, not je vais finir.

examples.english: I'm going to finish that tonight.

nodes.street_notes: The model regular -ir verb (-iss- in plural forms). c'est fini = it's over.

nodes.title: donner — to give

explanation.body: Donner is a clean -er verb. Every regular -er verb you learn later follows the same endings.

examples.french: je donne / tu donnes / ils donnent
examples.english: all three sound identical — the -s and -nt are silent

explanation.body: Use donner for everyday giving: handing something over, sharing information, or producing a result. Reserve offrir for wrapped gifts.

examples.english: Can you give me the address?

explanation.body: Donne-moi is the direct imperative — short and neutral, not rude. J'vais donner is futur proche at real speed; the vowel drops.

examples.english: I'll give it to him tomorrow.

explanation.body: Donner takes avoir in the past. Passé composé covers completed handovers; imparfait covers repeated or ongoing past giving.

examples.french: Il nous donnait toujours des conseils.
examples.english: He always used to give us advice.

nodes.street_notes: donne-moi = give me (imperative preview); ça donne quoi ? = how's it going / what's it like?

drills.english: I'm giving you a coffee.

nodes.title: Politeness conditionals: je voudrais, je pourrais, il faudrait

explanation.body: Every form here is the conditional tense. It adds distance between what you want and what you're demanding — that distance is politeness.

examples.french: Je veux un café. → Je voudrais un café.
examples.english: I want a coffee. → I'd like a coffee.

explanation.body: Each form does a different job. Mixing them up sounds odd.
bullets: voudrais / aimerais — ordering or requesting something for yourself
pourrais / pourriez — asking permission or making a suggestion
faudrait — 'we'd need to', without pointing at anyone
ce serait / ça serait — evaluating an option: 'that would be…'

examples.english: Could you repeat that? We'd need to book.

explanation.body: Il faudrait loses its subject in speech; faudrait alone is standard. Ce serait shifts to ça serait — ça is the default spoken pronoun for 'it/that'. Neither is sloppy; both are expected.

examples.english: We'd need to leave. That would be nice.

explanation.body: Don't use je veux in a shop — it reads as rude. Don't translate 'can I' as je peux; je pourrais is the polite default, just as 'could I' is softer than 'can I'.

examples.french: Je pourrais avoir l'addition ? J'aimerais voir la carte.
examples.english: Could I get the bill? I'd like to see the menu.

drills.english: I'd like a coffee.
I'd like to eat something.

drills.english: Could you help me? (informal)

nodes.title: croire — to believe / to think

explanation.body: Croire covers both 'to believe' and 'to think/reckon'. Don't default to penser — croire is what people actually say.
The stem is croi- before consonants, croy- before vowels. That one rule covers every irregular form.
bullets: croi-: je crois, tu crois, il croit, ils croient
croy-: vous croyez, je croyais, il croyait, ils croyaient

explanation.body: J'crois pas means 'I don't think so' — reaction-level, no full sentence needed. Add que for a clause.

examples.english: I don't think so. / I think so.

explanation.body: Opinions are usually ongoing states, so imparfait (croyais) is more natural in storytelling. Use passé composé for a single finished moment of believing.

examples.english: I thought it was true. (ongoing belief)

examples.french: J'ai cru ce qu'il m'a dit.
examples.english: I believed what he told me. (one moment)

nodes.street_notes: j'crois is one of the most common sentence-starters in spoken French — j'crois pas, j'crois que oui. tu crois → t'crois in fast speech.

drills.english: I think it's possible.

nodes.title: penser — to think

explanation.header: que vs à — get this right
explanation.body: Penser que introduces an opinion. Penser à means someone or something is on your mind. Swapping them is the main English-speaker error.
In real speech, j'pense que is as automatic as 'I think'. Negation drops ne entirely — you'll only hear j'pense pas.

examples.french: J'pense pas que c'est une bonne solution.
examples.english: I don't think it's a good solution.

explanation.header: One spoken stem, four identical sounds
explanation.body: All six present forms use pens-. Je, tu, il, and ils all sound like pense — context tells them apart.

examples.french: Il pense quoi ? On pense pareil.
examples.english: What does he think? We think the same.

explanation.body: Participle is pensé — drop -er, add -é. Futur proche and imparfait are fully regular.

examples.french: T'as pensé à ça ? J'pensais que t'étais là.
examples.english: Did you think about that? I thought you were there.

nodes.street_notes: j'pense is the everyday opinion opener — j'pense que c'est bon. penser à quelqu'un = think about; penser que = think that.

nodes.title: passer — to pass / to stop by

explanation.body: Passer takes être when no direct object follows. Add a direct object and it switches to avoir.

examples.french: On a passé une heure là-bas.

explanation.body: Passer covers passing by, dropping in, and spending time. Context decides the meaning.
These three phrases are ready-made. Learn them whole.
bullets: j'passe te voir — I'll drop in on you
ça passe — it's acceptable / it fits
ça va passer — it'll pass (pain, a phase)

explanation.body: Je drops its vowel before a consonant cluster. The rest follows standard ne-drop rules.

examples.english: I'll stop by tomorrow, does that work?

nodes.street_notes: je suis passé = I stopped by (être when moving); j'ai passé une bonne soirée (avoir when it takes an object). j'passe te voir = I'll drop in on you. ça passe = it's acceptable / it fits.

nodes.title: arriver — to arrive / to manage / to happen
nodes.english: to arrive / to manage / to happen

explanation.body: Arriver means to arrive, to happen, and to manage to do something. The third meaning trips up English speakers every time.

examples.english: I can't manage to open this.

explanation.body: Like aller and partir, arriver uses être in the passé composé. The past participle agrees with the subject.
bullets: woman: elle est arrivée
group: on est arrivés

explanation.body: J'arrive ! means 'coming!' — say it every time someone calls you. J'arrive pas à replaces je ne peux pas when something just won't work.

examples.english: I can't manage to get up.

nodes.street_notes: j'arrive ! = coming! — said constantly. ça arrive = it happens / no big deal. j'arrive pas à... = I can't manage to... (the everyday 'I can't').

nodes.title: rester — to stay

explanation.body: This is a false friend. 'To rest' is se reposer — a different verb entirely.
In third person, rester means 'there is/are ... left'. This has nothing to do with staying.

examples.french: Il reste du pain ? — Il en reste deux.
examples.english: Is there any bread left? — There are two left.

explanation.body: Rester uses être, not avoir. The past participle agrees with the subject.

examples.french: Elle est restée. On est restés.

explanation.body: Je drops its e before a consonant. Tu es resté collapses to t'es resté in real speech.

examples.french: J'reste là. T'es resté combien de temps ?
examples.english: I'm staying here. How long did you stay?

nodes.street_notes: false friend: rester = stay, never 'rest'. j'reste là = I'm staying here. il reste... = there's ... left (il en reste ? = is there any left?).

nodes.title: aider — to help

explanation.body: Aider takes its object directly — no 'à' before a person. Add 'à + infinitive' only when naming the task.

examples.french: Je l'aide à trouver une solution.
examples.english: I'm helping him find a solution.

explanation.body: Drop -er, add the endings. Je, tu, il, and on all sound identical: aide. The -ent in ils aident is silent.

examples.french: j'aide, t'aides, il aide, on aide

explanation.body: Tu aides contracts to t'aides. Real spoken requests use tu peux m'aider ?, not inversion.
Use passé composé for a completed act. Use imparfait for repeated or ongoing help in the past.

nodes.street_notes: j'aide by formal elision. tu aides → t'aides. Direct object, no preposition: tu peux m'aider ? = can you help me?

nodes.title: regarder — to watch / to look at

explanation.body: English says 'look at' or 'watch'. French says regarder + object, nothing in between. Adding 'à' after regarder is the classic English-speaker error.
Je, tu, il, and on all sound identical. The -ent in ils regardent is also silent — you only hear a new ending at vous regardez.

examples.french: j'regarde / tu regardes / il regarde / on regarde

explanation.body: Alone, regarde ! means 'look!' or 'check this out.' Friends use it constantly, also to open a point — like English 'look—' before an argument.
Use j'ai regardé for a finished viewing. Use je regardais for background — what was on when something else happened.

examples.french: Je regardais la télé quand il est arrivé.
examples.english: I was watching TV when he arrived.

nodes.street_notes: no 'at': regarder quelque chose directly — j'regarde la télé. regarde ! alone = look! — constant between friends.

nodes.title: trouver — to find

explanation.body: Trouver means to find a thing and to find/think something is a certain way. English speakers reach for penser — don't.
On trouve, il trouve, and ils trouvent are identical out loud. Context tells you which is which.

examples.french: on trouve / il trouve / ils trouvent

explanation.body: J'trouve que is your second opinion opener after j'crois que. Tu trouves ? alone means 'you think so?'

examples.french: J'trouve que c'est chaud. — Tu trouves ?
examples.english: I think it's rough. — You think so?
I don't think so. / I can't find it.

explanation.body: Passé composé is j'ai trouvé — fully regular. Use imparfait for past opinions; futur proche for plans.

nodes.title: attendre — to wait (for)

explanation.body: Drop -re, add -s, -s, nothing, -ez, -ent. Entendre and perdre follow the exact same pattern.

examples.french: j'attends, tu attends, il attend, on attend

explanation.body: French waits things directly. Adding 'pour' is the classic English-speaker error.
Said solo, it's filler-level frequent between friends. The past is fully regular: j'ai attendu.

nodes.street_notes: j'attends is already elided in formal French; tu attends → t'attends. Attends ! alone is the universal 'hang on'. attendre takes its object with no preposition — j'attends le bus, never 'pour'.

nodes.title: appeler — to call

explanation.body: Appeler doubles the l when the ending is silent, keeps one l when the ending is pronounced. Double l forces the open [ɛ] sound the silent ending needs.
bullets: Silent ending → double l: j'appelle, tu appelles, ils appellent
Pronounced ending → single l: vous appelez

explanation.body: Je t'appelle means 'I'll call you' as often as 'I'm calling you.' Don't reach for futur proche — the present already handles it.
Tu appelles compresses to t'appelles. The passé composé is fully regular.

nodes.street_notes: double-l where the ending is silent (j'appelle), single l where it's pronounced (vous appelez). j'appelle = I'll call — présent does the near future. je t'appelle = I'll call you.

nodes.title: connaître — to know (people, places)

explanation.body: Connaître = familiarity with people, places, things. Savoir = facts or how-to. English uses one word for both, so this trips up every learner.

examples.french: Je connais Paris. Je sais qu'il fait froid.
examples.english: I know Paris (I've been). I know it's cold (fact).

examples.french: Tu connais Marie ? Tu sais où elle habite ?
examples.english: Do you know Marie? Do you know where she lives?

explanation.body: The present stem is connais- throughout — except il/elle gets a circumflex: connaît. That's the only irregularity.

examples.french: je connais / il connaît / ils connaissent
examples.english: three written shapes, one pronunciation pattern

explanation.body: J'connais is the spoken norm. J'connais pas covers 'I don't know' for people and places — use chais pas for facts.

examples.english: You know it? — the standard recommendation opener.

explanation.body: Connaître describes a state, so imparfait is the default. Use passé composé only for the moment of meeting or discovery.

examples.english: I met Marie in Paris. (single event)

nodes.street_notes: connaître = know people/places/things by acquaintance; savoir = know facts/how-to. j'connais pas = don't know it/them. tu connais ? = you know it? — the recommendation opener.

nodes.title: porter — to wear / to carry

explanation.body: Porter covers both. Context decides which meaning: a garment means wear, a bag means carry.
English speakers use mettre (to put on) when they mean porter. Mettre is the action; porter is the result — having it on.

examples.french: Elle met son manteau. → Elle porte son manteau.
examples.english: She puts on her coat. → She's wearing her coat.

explanation.body: Je, tu, il, on all sound identical: port. Only vous portez and ils portent differ.

examples.french: j'porte, tu portes, il porte, on porte

explanation.body: Je drops its e before a consonant. Ne disappears in negation. T'as porté is the street passé composé for tu.

examples.french: Le beige, ça se porte beaucoup.
examples.english: Beige is very much in style.

nodes.street_notes: wear and carry are the same verb — context decides. j'porte jamais de noir = I never wear black. ça se porte = people wear that.

nodes.title: sortir — to go out / to leave

explanation.body: Both translate as 'to leave', but they don't overlap. Sortir = out of an enclosed space or your routine. Partir = leaving a place or person behind.

examples.french: Je sors du bureau à six heures.
examples.english: I leave the office at six.

explanation.body: Drop -tir and the stem consonant for je/tu. Keep the t for il, on, ils. This is identical to partir.

examples.french: je sors, tu sors / il sort, on sort, ils sortent
examples.english: I leave, you leave / he leaves, we leave, they leave

explanation.body: Sortir builds its passé composé with être, not avoir. The participle is sorti; it agrees with the subject in writing.
Sortir avec quelqu'un means 'to date someone'. Context makes the sense clear.

examples.english: Did you date her for long?

nodes.street_notes: same shape as partir: sors/sort split. on sort ce soir ? = we going out tonight? je suis sorti hier = I went out last night. sortir avec quelqu'un = to date someone.

nodes.title: comprendre — to understand

explanation.header: It's just prendre with a prefix
explanation.body: Comprendre is prendre plus a prefix — the forms are identical. The one surprise: ils comprennent doubles the n and adds a syllable.

examples.french: je comprends, il comprend, ils comprennent
examples.english: I understand, he understands, they understand

explanation.body: English adds 'it' or 'that'; French drops it unless the object is specific. J'comprends pas is the survival phrase — use it as-is.

examples.french: J'comprends pas. / Je comprends la situation.
examples.english: I don't understand. / I understand the situation.

explanation.body: The past participle compris works as a spoken check. These two chunks are higher-frequency than any full sentence.
Each tense maps to a specific moment.
bullets: Present → live moment: j'comprends pas
Imparfait → ongoing past state: je comprenais rien
Passé composé → sudden click: là j'ai compris
Futur proche → reassurance: tu vas comprendre

nodes.street_notes: prendre with a prefix — same forms exactly. j'comprends pas = the survival phrase. compris ? / c'est compris = got it?

nodes.title: entendre — to hear

explanation.body: Entendre follows the standard -re pattern: drop the -re, add -s, -s, nothing, -ez, -ent. The spoken stem entend covers je, tu, il, and on — one sound, four spellings.

examples.french: j'entends, t'entends, il entend, on entend
examples.english: four spellings, one spoken stem: entend

explanation.body: Entendre is passive — sound reaches you. Écouter is active — you direct your attention. Swapping them is the main English-speaker mistake.

examples.french: J'entends la musique mais j'écoute pas.
examples.english: I can hear the music but I'm not listening.

explanation.body: Like its twin attendre, entendre takes its object directly — no preposition between verb and noun.
J'entends rien means 'I can't hear anything' — on calls, in loud places, everywhere. Rien drops the ne; nothing else changes.

examples.french: J'entends rien, tu peux parler plus fort ?
examples.english: I can't hear anything, can you speak louder?

nodes.street_notes: attendre's twin — same -re shape. j'entends rien = I can't hear anything. entendre = hear (passive), écouter = listen (active).

nodes.title: demander — to ask (for)

explanation.body: Demander means 'to ask' or 'to ask for'. Never use it to mean 'to demand'.
The thing you're asking for goes straight after the verb — no 'pour'. To name the person, add 'à'.

examples.english: I'm asking Paul for the bill.

explanation.body: The reflexive j'me demande is its own fixed chunk. It means 'I wonder', not 'I ask myself'.
Demander is fully regular -er. On the street, je drops its 'e' before a consonant.

nodes.title: chercher — to look for

explanation.body: 'For' is already inside chercher. Don't add pour — the object follows directly, like with attendre.
Je, tu, il, on, and ils all land on the same spoken syllable: cherch. Only vous sounds different.

examples.french: j'cherche / tu cherches / il cherche / on cherche / ils cherchent
examples.english: five spellings, one sound — then: vous cherchez

explanation.body: Je cherche… is how you ask for directions or flag down a shop assistant. It's more natural than a direct question.

examples.french: Je cherche la rue de Rivoli.

nodes.title: laisser — to leave / to let

explanation.body: Laisser covers both 'leave something behind' and 'let someone do something'. For 'let', use laisser + person + infinitive — no extra word.
Laisse tomber means 'forget it / drop it'. Treat it as a fixed chunk, not a translation.

examples.english: Forget it, it's not a big deal.

explanation.body: Je laisse shortens to j'laisse. Tu as laissé becomes t'as laissé. Ne drops: tu laisses pas is the spoken norm.
Laisser follows the standard -er pattern exactly. The participle laissé is predictable.

nodes.title: écouter — to listen (to)

explanation.body: English says 'listen TO'; French drops that preposition entirely. Adding à or pour is the automatic English-speaker error.
Écoute or écoutez at the start of a sentence means 'look' or 'listen'. It signals importance, not literal hearing.
Écouter is fully regular -er. Street speech only adds two changes: drop ne, and contract tu to t'.

nodes.title: perdre — to lose

explanation.body: Drop -re, add -s, -s, nothing, -ez, -ent. Je perds, tu perds, il perd all sound identical in speech.

nodes.title: tenir — to hold

explanation.body: Tenir means 'to hold'. It also means 'to care about' with à, and tiens ! signals handovers or surprise.

explanation.body: Tenir and venir share identical endings. The only difference: tenir takes avoir in the passé composé, not être.
bullets: je/tu/il: tiens / tiens / tient
ils: tiennent (double n — stems doubles)
past participle: tenu (with avoir)

explanation.body: Je tiens drops to j'tiens in flow. Tu as tenu becomes t'as tenu. Ils tiennent already sounds natural — no further change.

nodes.title: vivre — to live

explanation.body: Both mean 'to live', but habiter is just your address. Vivre adds the sense of experiencing a place or a life.

examples.french: J'vis à Paris. / J'habite à Paris.
examples.english: Both work for stating where you live.
Life is good here. (habiter sounds wrong here)

explanation.body: The present stem drops from vivre to vi-. The -s/-s/-t endings are standard -re; only the stem is odd. Vivent keeps the full stem.

examples.french: Il vit seul. Ils vivent ensemble.
examples.english: He lives alone. They live together.

explanation.body: The past participle is vécu — irregular, memorise it once. Use it for a defined period that's over. English says 'lived' for both states and events; French uses vécu only for those closed phases.

examples.french: J'ai vécu deux ans en France.
examples.english: I lived in France for two years.
What experiences have you lived through?

nodes.street_notes: j'vis à Paris = I live in Paris — self-introduction core (habiter works too). j'ai vécu deux ans en France = I lived there two years — vécu for life chapters.

nodes.title: essayer — to try

explanation.header: Always add DE before a verb
explanation.body: Essayer needs de before any following infinitive. Drop it and the sentence breaks — this trips up English speakers every time.
When the ending is silent, y changes to i. When the ending is pronounced, y stays. It's a sound rule, not a person rule.

examples.english: silent ending → i / silent ending → i / pronounced ending → y

explanation.body: In real speech, futur proche handles 'I'll try'. It works as both a promise and a soft commitment.

examples.french: J'vais essayer, chais pas si ça marche.
examples.english: I'll try, not sure if it'll work.

explanation.body: One finished attempt → passé composé. Repeated or ongoing tries → imparfait.

examples.french: J'essayais de le joindre mais c'était pas possible.
examples.english: I kept trying to reach him but it wasn't possible.

nodes.street_notes: y → i where the ending is silent (j'essaie), y kept where pronounced (vous essayez). essayer DE + infinitive: j'essaie d'apprendre. j'vais essayer = I'll try.

nodes.title: travailler — to work

explanation.body: Strip -er from travailler, add the standard endings. The stem is travail-.

examples.french: je travaille, tu travailles, il travaille, ils travaillent

explanation.body: Je, tu, il, and on all sound identical out loud. Don't pronounce the -ent on ils travaillent — it's silent.

examples.french: J'travaille, t'travailles, il travaille, on travaille

explanation.body: Each tense marks a different work moment:
bullets: présent — today's schedule or habit: j'travaille demain
futur proche — any upcoming plan: j'vais travailler ce soir
passé composé — a finished stint: t'as travaillé hier ?
imparfait — what work was like: j'travaillais jusqu'à vingt heures

examples.french: T'as travaillé hier ? — Ouais, j'travaillais jusqu'à vingt heures.
examples.english: Did you work yesterday? — Yeah, I was working until eight.

explanation.body: Bosser and taffer mean the same thing and are common. Recognise them; produce travailler — it works in every register.

examples.french: Tu travailles où ? — J'travaille au centre.
examples.english: Where do you work? — I work in the centre.

nodes.street_notes: the small-talk verb: tu travailles où ? / j'travaille demain. Street synonyms you'll hear: bosser, taffer — recognize them, say travailler.

nodes.title: rentrer — to go home / to get back
nodes.english: to go home / to get back

explanation.body: Rentrer means to go back to your base — home is implied. Never add 'à la maison'; it's almost never said.
Rentrer is a movement-to-a-place verb, so passé composé uses être. The past participle agrees with the subject.
bullets: one man: rentré
a group: rentrés

examples.french: Je suis rentré tard. On est rentrés ensemble.
examples.english: I got home late. We got home together.

explanation.body: Je drops its e before the r: j'rentre. Être passé composé contracts the same way as every être verb.

examples.french: J'rentre. T'es rentré à quelle heure ?
examples.english: I'm heading home. What time did you get back?

explanation.body: One completed trip = passé composé. Ongoing background action = imparfait.

examples.french: Je rentrais quand ça a sonné.
examples.english: I was heading home when it rang.

nodes.street_notes: 'home' is built in: j'rentre = I'm heading home. tu rentres quand ? = when are you getting back? je suis rentré tard = I got home late (être).

nodes.title: commencer — to start

explanation.body: Commencer is a regular -er verb except one thing: ç appears only before the letter a, to keep the c soft. The imparfait ending -ais starts with a, so it triggers ç. Everywhere else, plain c.

examples.english: I start / I was starting — one letter changes, nothing else

explanation.body: When commencer links to another verb, you need à. Drop it and the sentence is wrong. English uses 'to' or '-ing'; French always uses à.
Je contracts before a consonant. Ça commence quand? is natural word order — inversion sounds formal here.

nodes.street_notes: commencer À + infinitive: ça commence à m'énerver = it's starting to annoy me. ça commence quand ? = when does it start? (The ç spelling only shows up before a: je commençais.)

nodes.title: oublier — to forget

explanation.body: Drop the -r, add standard endings — no surprises. One exception: vous oubliiez has two i's because the stem ends in -i and the imparfait ending adds another.

examples.english: two i's, one sound — looks odd, follows the rule

explanation.body: Forgetting is almost always a one-off event, not a background state. Use passé composé by default.
English says 'I forgot about it.' French skips 'about' and uses the direct object pronoun directly.
Learn these as fixed units — they come up constantly.
bullets: j'oublie tout le temps — I always forget (self-deprecating, everyday)
t'as oublié ? — did you forget? (friendly accusation)
oublie ! — forget it (dismissive, sharper than laisse tomber)

nodes.street_notes: j'ai oublié — the daily confession. j'oublie tout le temps = I always forget. oublie ! = forget it (sharper than laisse tomber).

nodes.title: boire — to drink

explanation.body: Boire splits between boi- and buv-. The jump is irregular but consistent — once you know which form gets which stem, it holds.
bullets: boi-: je bois, tu bois, il boit, ils boivent
buv-: vous buvez, je buvais, il buvait, ils buvaient

explanation.body: Bu is short and irregular — memorise it. It pairs with avoir, not être.

examples.french: T'as bu quoi ? On a bu un verre.
examples.english: What did you drink? We had a drink.

explanation.body: Boire covers all drinking — coffee, water, alcohol. Boire un verre is the fixed phrase for a social drink. Tu bois quoi ? is the standard bar opener; learn it as one chunk.

examples.french: Tu bois quoi ? J'bois un café.
examples.english: What are you having? I'm having a coffee.

explanation.body: The boi- stem starts with a consonant, so je always drops its vowel in fast speech.

examples.english: I don't drink anything in the morning.
I was drinking, I'm going to drink

nodes.street_notes: bois/boit for everyone singular, then the stem jumps: buvez, boivent, j'ai bu. tu bois quoi ? = what are you drinking? — the bar opener. boire un verre = have a drink.

present.label: Présent
present.note: French has one present where English has three. Je mange covers 'I eat', 'I'm eating', and 'I do eat'. There is no -ing form to build. Add a time word and it covers the near future too.

contrasts.point: One form carries both English presents — context does the rest.
With a concrete time, the présent covers the near future on its own.

passe_compose.label: Passé composé
passe_compose.note: The tense for events: things that happened, finished, and moved the story forward. It answers 'what happened?'. Built from avoir or être plus the participle — which is why the contraction lands on the auxiliary (t'as mangé), not the verb.

contrasts.b_english: I was eating when you called.
contrasts.point: Event vs background: the passé composé is the thing that happened; the imparfait is what was already going on.
A bounded event takes passé composé; an unbounded backdrop takes imparfait.
Same verb, different question: outcome as an event vs description of how it was.

imparfait.label: Imparfait
imparfait.note: The tense for background: how things were, what used to happen, what was mid-flight when the event hit. If the passé composé is the photo, the imparfait is the scenery it was taken in. English gives you two reliable signals: 'was …-ing' and 'used to'.

contrasts.point: Mid-action vs completed: the imparfait leaves the film running.

contrasts.a_french: On allait au café tous les matins.
contrasts.a_english: We used to go to the café every morning.
contrasts.b_french: On est allés au café ce matin.
contrasts.b_english: We went to the café this morning.
contrasts.point: Habit in the past is imparfait territory; the one-time trip is an event.
Descriptions and states live in the imparfait; happenings live in the passé composé.

futur_proche.label: Futur proche
futur_proche.note: Aller + infinitive is how spoken French handles the future — plans, predictions, things about to happen. It is the English 'going to', built the same way, and in conversation it covers nearly everything the written future tense does. If you can conjugate aller, you can say anything in the future.

contrasts.point: Same meaning; the futur proche is the spoken norm, the futur simple reads as written or formal register.
The everyday reassurance phrases are all futur proche — fixed frames worth owning whole.
